🇸🇰 SK

ECB SAFE Survey — Wave 38

Slovakia · Euro Area · Germany  |  Generated 01 Jul 2026

Július Koller — Pre každú príležitosť... osviežujúci národný podnik. (UFO) (1978)

Executive Summary

  • Cost-revenue squeeze: Labour costs rose for a net 84% of Slovak firms while turnover and profits fell for a net 26% and 40% respectively, intensifying margin pressure (business_situation).
  • Credit line stress: The bank loan gap improved but the credit line gap widened sharply to +14.8pp, driven by a 9.2pp surge in need, signalling liquidity strain (financing_gap).
  • Financing outlook darkens: A net 5% of firms expected bank loan availability to deteriorate, reversing prior positive sentiment and aligning with EA trends (availability_expectations).
  • Investment plans defy constraints: Despite weaker revenue expectations, a net 15% of firms plan higher fixed investment, outpacing the EA’s net 9% (outlook).

Contents

  • Financing Conditions
    • Slovak firms swung to net interest rate tightening after prior-wave easing
    • Credit line stress in Slovakia surpassed euro area as demand sharply rose

    • Slovak firms see stronger upside inflation risks than euro area peers
    • Slovak firms rate access to finance more pressing while other pressures ease

Financing Conditions

Slovak firms swung to net interest rate tightening after prior-wave easing

Changes in Terms and Conditions of Bank Financing (Q10)

  • A net 5% of Slovak firms (n=62) reported tighter interest rates on bank loans — a reversal of 13.8 pp from the net 8% easing in the previous quarter — though still well below the EA's net 27% tightening (n=1,892), indicating a shift toward less favorable financing conditions for Slovak borrowers.
  • Non-interest financing costs (charges, fees and commissions) tightened for a net 41% of Slovak firms (n=60), easing marginally by 1.8 pp from the prior quarter's net 42%, remaining above the EA's net 37% (n=1,902) and close to Germany's net 49%, putting pressure on firms' cost structures.
  • Collateral requirements tightened for a net 1% of Slovak firms (n=62) and other terms (guarantees, covenants and procedures) for a net 8% (n=60), both far below Germany's net 32% and net 33% respectively, suggesting Slovak banks' non-price conditions remain comparatively contained.

* Only firms that have used or applied for this type of financing in the past are asked this question. A lower n relative to the total sample is by design and does not indicate a data quality issue — see ECB SAFE methodology for details.

Credit line stress in Slovakia surpassed euro area as demand sharply rose

Financing Need vs Availability Gap (Q5/Q9)

  • For bank loans, Slovak firms remained less stressed than the EA: the gap stood at -4.0pp (need: -2.6pp, availability: +1.4pp, n=254), narrowing from -7.6pp in the previous quarter as need edged up by 3.6pp while availability held steady — suggesting improved conditions relative to peers.
  • The credit line gap widened sharply to +14.8pp (need: +12.3pp, n=278; availability: -2.5pp) — above the EA's +12.6pp and up from +4.8pp the prior quarter — driven almost entirely by a 9.2pp surge in need rather than tighter supply, putting pressure on firms' liquidity buffers.
  • Trade credit stress eased: a net 7.1% of Slovak firms (n=150) reported rising need against only +0.8pp availability improvement, leaving a gap of +6.3pp, down from +9.8pp the prior quarter and now 1.9pp below the EA's +8.2pp — indicating some relief in alternative financing sources.

* Only firms that have used or applied for this type of financing in the past are asked this question. A lower n relative to the total sample is by design and does not indicate a data quality issue — see ECB SAFE methodology for details.

Slovak firms increasingly expect bank loan availability to deteriorate ahead

Expected Availability of External Financing (Q23)

  • A net 5% of Slovak firms (n=130) expected bank loan availability to deteriorate over the coming period — implying tighter access to financing for a small but growing share of firms.
  • Slovak firms' pessimism on bank loans now slightly exceeds the EA average — where a net 4% of firms (n=2,948) also expected deterioration — suggesting broadly shared tightening expectations with a modest local bias.
  • Both Slovakia and the EA have shifted into negative territory on bank loan availability expectations, reversing net positive balances held as recently as wave 35 — signalling a sustained turn in forward-looking credit sentiment across the region.

* Only firms that have used or applied for this type of financing in the past are asked this question. A lower n relative to the total sample is by design and does not indicate a data quality issue — see ECB SAFE methodology for details.

† Observations with fewer than 10 valid responses in a given wave × country × sub-item cell are excluded from the chart; gaps in series indicate insufficient data for that period.

🤖 This section includes data retrieved by an AI agent querying the SAFE database directly during report generation.

Slovak firms lean heavily on working capital and product development financing

Purpose of Financing (Q6A)

  • A dominant 55.7% of Slovak firms (n=229) cited inventory and working capital as a financing purpose, broadly unchanged from 55.8% in the previous wave and well above the euro area's 39.5% — highlighting their persistent reliance on short-term liquidity needs.
  • The share of Slovak firms financing the development and launching of new products or services rose to 32.8% (n=227), up from 29.9% in the previous wave, outpacing the euro area's 20.2% by a margin of 12.6 percentage points — underscoring their focus on innovation-driven growth.
  • Slovak firms citing hiring and training of employees as a financing purpose rose to 31.1% (n=227), up 4.2 pp from 26.9% in the previous wave, compared with 24.3% across the euro area — while fixed investment financing eased 6.1 pp to 34.2% (n=229) — suggesting a shift toward labor-related expenses over long-term capital expenditures.

🤖 This section includes data retrieved by an AI agent querying the SAFE database directly during report generation.

Slovak firms report easing bank willingness amid worsening economic outlook

Factors Affecting Access to External Financing (Q11)

  • A net 8% of Slovak firms (n=306) reported that banks' willingness to provide credit improved, easing 2.6 pp from a net 10% in the previous quarter, yet still above the EA's net 6% and contrasting with Germany's net 4% deterioration — suggesting a slight improvement in credit supply conditions.
  • A net 44% of Slovak firms (n=442) reported a deterioration in the general economic outlook — worsening 6.1 pp from a net 38% in the prior quarter — markedly exceeding the EA's net 26% deterioration and acting as a significant macro-level drag on financing conditions.
  • Access to public financial support deteriorated for a net 34% of Slovak firms (n=298), widening by 8.8 pp from a net 25% in the previous quarter, the sharpest quarterly deterioration among the three economies and more than double the EA's net 16% — putting further strain on firms' external financing options.

Economic Situation of Firms

Slovak firms reported sharply falling turnover and profits amid surging costs

Business Situation Indicators (Q2)

  • A net 26% of Slovak firms (n=229) reported declining turnover — where more firms said revenues fell than rose — worsening by 11.6 pp from a net 15% decline in the previous quarter, far below the EA's near-flat net 1% gain, signalling weaker revenue prospects for firms.
  • A net 40% of Slovak firms (n=229) reported falling profits, deteriorating 17.4 pp from a net 22% decline in the prior quarter, more than double the EA's net 17% profit contraction, squeezing financial headroom for investment and operations.
  • Labour costs rose for a net 84% of Slovak firms (n=229), climbing 21.8 pp from a net 62% in the previous quarter and outpacing the already elevated EA net of 63%, pointing to intensifying cost pressure across the Slovak economy.

Slovak turnover expectations fell sharply, diverging from rising EA optimism

Expected Changes in Turnover and Investment (Q26)

  • A net 7% of Slovak firms (n=228) expected turnover to increase over the coming quarter, falling 14 pp from the prior wave's net 21% — while the EA rose to a net 26% (n=5,266), widening the gap to 19 pp, suggesting a growing competitiveness concern for Slovak firms.
  • Slovak fixed investment expectations bucked the turnover trend: a net 15% of firms (n=228) anticipated higher investment, rising 4 pp from the prior wave's net 11% and outpacing the EA's net 9% (n=5,266), which itself eased 3 pp.
  • The divergence in turnover expectations — Slovakia at a net 7% versus the EA's net 26% — signals a weakening domestic demand outlook for Slovak firms relative to the broader euro area.

Slovak firms expect input prices to outpace selling prices by over 4 percentage points

Price, Wage and Employment Expectations (Q31/Q34)

  • Slovak firms (n=418) expected average selling prices to rise 4.27% over the next 12 months, easing 0.36 pp from the previous quarter's 4.63% — yet still outpacing the EA's 3.63%, suggesting potential margin compression for Slovak firms.
  • Slovak firms (n=417) anticipated production input prices rising 8.31% — nearly double their 4.27% selling price expectation and well above the EA's 5.84% — while wage expectations of 4.21% (n=441, easing 1.28 pp from the prior quarter's 5.49%) add further margin pressure.
  • Slovak firms' 12-month inflation expectations rose to 4.97% (n=189, up 0.18 pp from the prior quarter), above the EA's 3.94%, with five-year expectations climbing to 8.57% (n=163, up 1.51 pp) — the largest horizon increase across all three economies, signalling heightened long-term cost concerns.

† Observations with fewer than 10 valid responses in a given wave × country × sub-item cell are excluded from the chart; gaps in series indicate insufficient data for that period.

Slovak firms see stronger upside inflation risks than euro area peers

Inflation Risk Outlook (Q33)

  • A net 59% of Slovak firms (n=229) saw inflation risks skewed to the upside — meaning they expected inflation in five years to come in higher than currently anticipated — rising 5.8 pp from a net 53% in the previous wave, implying heightened pricing pressure for Slovak firms over the medium term.
  • Slovak firms' upside inflation risk balance exceeded the euro area's net 55% (n=5,278), suggesting greater local price persistence, even as the EA itself rose a sharper 9.9 pp from the prior wave, underscoring divergent inflation expectations within the monetary union.
  • Germany recorded a net 51% upside inflation risk balance, rising 9.7 pp from the previous wave, leaving Slovakia as the most inflation-risk-tilted of the three economies in wave 38 — a position that may signal tighter cost conditions for Slovak businesses relative to peers.

† Observations with fewer than 10 valid responses in a given wave × country × sub-item cell are excluded from the chart; gaps in series indicate insufficient data for that period.

Slovak firms rate access to finance more pressing while other pressures ease

Most Pressing Business Problems (Q0B)

  • Slovak firms (n=223) rated access to finance at an average pressingness score of 3.91 out of 10, rising 0.19 points from the previous quarter — the only business problem among Slovak firms to increase, contrasting with the EA's easing to 3.99, which suggests growing constraints on growth plans.
  • The most pressing problem for Slovak firms (n=228) remained availability of skilled staff or experienced managers at 6.07, though this eased by 0.50 points from the prior quarter and remained below the EA average of 6.51.
  • Regulation ranked as Slovakia's second most pressing concern (n=225, score 6.18), rising 0.12 points from the previous quarter and broadly matching the EA's score of 5.89 — while costs of production or labour eased slightly to 6.19 (Δ −0.15 pp).

Source: ECB SAFE microdata. Net balance = % reporting increase minus % reporting decrease. Positive = tightening/rising (adverse for firms unless noted). Negative = easing/falling.
